lines.original: Pakki aa jabaan, jeeb kare na slip
lines.translated: My tongue's steady; it doesn't slip.
lines.note: Pakki/jeeb both refer to 'tongue' or speech — the line asserts the speaker won't misspeak; 'slip' is an English loanword used in the original.

lines.original: Kurta ae kaala, beeba yaaran di drip
lines.translated: The kurta is black, the girl has her friends' drip.
lines.note: "beeba" = girl (affectionate). "yaaran di drip" literally "friends' drip" — "drip" is slang for style/fashion; conservative reading: she has the style of her friends or is stylish like her friends.

lines.original: Yatch te maal phi si chali sip-sip
lines.translated: On the yacht, the goods were moving, sip‑sip.
lines.note: 'maal' is slang and can mean goods, valuables, or attractive people — ambiguous here. 'sip‑sip' is an onomatopoeic reference to sipping drinks.

lines.original: Saala quarter milli ch peya europe trip
lines.translated: Damn, a Europe trip cost a quarter million.
lines.note: “Saala” is an expletive filler (colloquial 'damn'). “quarter milli” means 'quarter million' (currency unspecified); 'ch peya' indicates it was spent or went into that amount.

lines.original: Fir tadkadi re thalle ho gayi muchh jeh ni
lines.translated: Then the tadkadi fell down beneath; there wasn't even a moustache.
lines.note: “Tadkadi” is ambiguous here (likely something like a spark/sizzle/flash); “muchh” means moustache and can symbolize pride or bravado. Translation stays literal because the exact colloquial sense is unclear.

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re baaki duniyan russ jaye ni
lines.translated: If our God is pleased, let the rest of the world be upset.
lines.transliteration: Saddā rabb rāzī re, bākī duniyā̃ rus jāe nī
lines.note: “rabb raaji” = ‘May God be pleased’; “russ jaye” = ‘may (others) get upset/sulk’; the final “ni” is an emphatic particle.

lines.original: Sadda rab raaji rahe
lines.translated: May God be pleased with us
lines.note: A short blessing/wish asking that God remain pleased or content with the singer and their people.

lines.original: Assi nahiyon kise naal number bataune
lines.translated: We don't give our number to anyone

lines.original: Beeba assi nahiyon kise muhre number banaune
lines.translated: Beeba, we don't share our number with anyone.
lines.note: Beeba is an affectionate address meaning 'girl'/'dear girl'. 'Number banaune' is ambiguous — it can mean giving/sharing a phone number or 'making a scene/putting on a show'; here it's rendered as 'share our number' based on the preceding line's context.

lines.original: Hatth naa chadde ni bas neeli chatt aala
lines.translated: Don't let go of my hand, just the one with the blue roof.
lines.note: Literal: 'neeli chatt aala' means 'the one with the blue roof' (could refer to a house, vehicle, or something marked by a blue roof). 'Hatth naa chadde ni' is an emphatic 'don't let go of (my) hand.'

lines.original: Main keha rab nu manauna assi bande ni manaune
lines.translated: I said I'd appease God — we don't appease people
lines.note: ‘Manauna’ means to appease/convince/please; the line stresses refusing to bow to or flatter other people.

lines.original: Machde machauna dinda single ni f
lines.translated: I let them make a fuss — I'm not single, f
lines.note: Literal, conservative render. 'dinda' (lets/gives) subject is implicit. The trailing 'f' is ambiguous (possible typo/abbreviation); kept as in original rather than guessing its meaning.

lines.original: Ohdi raja bina hai ni single breath
lines.translated: There isn't a single breath without her king.
lines.note: Literal, conservative reading. 'Ohdi' = 'her' (could be 'his' depending on context), 'raja' = 'king' (often used for a lover/important man), 'ni' is an emphatic Punjabi particle, and 'single breath' is English here meaning 'not even one breath/moment.'

lines.original: Karna ni kaara rakhe taar mexico aale
lines.translated: He won't do it; those from Mexico wear black threads.
lines.note: Literal rendering. The line is ambiguous: kaara can mean 'black' or 'kara' (a wrist bangle) and taar can mean 'thread' or 'wire'; I conservatively rendered it as 'black threads'. 'Mexico aale' = 'those from Mexico.'

lines.original: Rotiyan khawaunda bai ayeh italian chef
lines.translated: He feeds rotis, bro — he's an Italian chef.
lines.note: “bai” is slang for 'bro'; the line contrasts plain Punjabi rotis with an 'Italian chef'—an ironic or boastful image.

lines.original: Hatth ohda sir te
lines.translated: His hand on his head

lines.original: Yaar kidda fir rukk jaye ni
lines.translated: Friend, how could I stop then?
lines.note: ‘Yaar’ is a vocative 'friend/buddy'; 'ni' is an emphatic Punjabi particle (colloquial).

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased

lines.original: Baaki duniyan russ jaye ni
lines.translated: Let the rest of the world be upset

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased.

lines.original: Baaki duniyan russ jaye ni
lines.translated: Let the rest of the world be upset

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased.

lines.original: Mittraan ne paayi jehde khutti sun bole
lines.translated: My friends have set up those who speak nonsense.
lines.note: Conservative reading: 'paayi jehde' = 'set up/put in place those who'; 'khutti' is slangy/ambiguous—likely means 'nonsense' or 'fake talk'.

lines.original: Beeba vairi meri nok meri jutti sun bole
lines.translated: Dear girl — my enemy talks about the tip of my shoe
lines.note: ‘Beeba’ = ‘dear girl’ (glossary). ‘nok’ is ambiguous (literal: 'tip' or 'point'; can also imply a jab/insult); ‘sun bole’ conveys talking/gossiping.

lines.original: Jatt de asoolan ch ni dovein paase challan
lines.translated: In a jatt's code, I don't play both sides.
lines.note: ‘Jatt’ refers to a tough/proud Punjabi man (see glossary). 'Don't play both sides' means he won't be two-faced or take both sides.

lines.original: Tahiyon chaare paase aujle di tooti sun bole
lines.translated: So all around you hear Aujla's broken words.
lines.note: Aujla is the artist's name. 'chaare paase' = 'all around'; 'tooti' literally means 'broken' — here it likely refers to fragmented or harsh-sounding words/voice, which is somewhat ambiguous, so a literal reading is used. Transliteration omitted because the original is already in Latin script.

lines.original: Naam bole peak te ni sun laake kann
lines.translated: When my name is spoken, don't listen, even if you cup your ear.
lines.note: The phrase 'peak' in the original is ambiguous here (could mean 'at the peak' or be phonetic); I rendered a conservative, literal meaning focused on 'naam bole' and 'sun laake kann' ('don't listen even if you put your ear to listen').

lines.original: Chann jeha gabru chadhaunda phire chann
lines.translated: A moon-like young man struts around, moon
lines.note: 'gabru' means a young man; 'chann jeha' = 'like the moon'. The final 'chann' functions as a vocative/refrain.

lines.original: Yaar naal turde aa chadhe jivein chann
lines.translated: They walk with their friends, as if the moon had risen.
lines.note: 'Chann' (moon) is often used as a compliment in Punjabi, likening someone's looks or presence to the moon.

lines.original: Main keha rm gutt te aa thirty-zero-one
lines.translated: I said rm gutt is on the thirty-zero-one
lines.note: 'rm gutt' is unclear here and left as-is; 'thirty-zero-one' appears to be a numeric reference (possibly a firearm calibre or code).

lines.original: Oh sittda kitthe
lines.translated: Where's he sitting?

lines.original: Jehda buch jaye buch jaye ni
lines.translated: Whoever's spared, whoever's spared, oh
lines.note: 'ni' is an emphatic/vocative particle; 'buch jaye' here likely means 'is spared' or 'survives.'

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased
lines.note: 'rabb' = God; 'raaji' = pleased/satisfied; 're' is an emphatic particle — a short prayer/blessing asking that God be pleased with us.

lines.original: Baaki duniyan russ jaye ni
lines.translated: Let the rest of the world stay upset

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased
lines.note: rabb = God; raaji = pleased/content; 're' is an emphatic/vocative particle adding emphasis.

lines.original: Baaki duniyan russ jaye ni
lines.translated: Let the rest of the world be upset

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased with us

lines.original: Lagde ta ohne jih te sakk vi ni painda
lines.translated: It seems they're the kind who aren't even affected by a blow.
lines.note: ‘Lagde ta’ = 'it seems'. ‘ohne jih’ ≈ 'they are the kind (such as)', and ‘sakk’ here means 'blow/impact/effect' — so the line says they don't get affected even by a blow.

lines.original: Beeba vairi da farak sahnu kakh vi ni painda
lines.translated: The difference between a girl and an enemy doesn't matter to us at all.
lines.note: “Beeba” = girl/dear girl (glossary). “kakh vi ni painda” = doesn’t affect us / we don’t care at all—line expresses indifference to whether someone is a lover or an enemy.

lines.original: Bagge de munde di saale karde ne rees
lines.translated: The Bagge boys' bastards get jealous.
lines.note: 'saale' is a slang insult (literally 'brother-in-law' but used like 'bastards/jerks'); 'rees' means jealousy/grudge. 'Bagge' likely refers to a name or group identifier.

lines.original: Ni main ohton kadda note jithe atth vi ni painda
lines.translated: I pull out a bill from where even an eight won't fit.
lines.note: “Ni” is a filler address. “note” refers to a banknote/cash. “atth” (aath/eight) is ambiguous here—likely literal 'eight' used to emphasize how tight or stingy the place is; translation kept conservatively literal.

lines.original: Bachke rakaane sutte sher naa jaga di
lines.translated: Be careful — don't wake a sleeping lion.
lines.note: 'sutte sher' ('sleeping lion') is an idiom warning not to provoke someone dangerous or powerful.

lines.original: Jammeyan ni koyi nehde tehde tan dikha di
lines.translated: No one, since birth, has ever shown up this close.
lines.note: "Jammeyan ni koyi" literally means "no one since birth." "nehde tehde" conveys being near/close; "tan" is an emphatic particle. The exact nuance of "showed up this close" is ambiguous in the original, so this is a conservative, literal rendering.

lines.original: Mohde naal mittaran di khainge kithon ni sahnu
lines.translated: They'll mess with my friends in front of me; I won't tolerate that from anywhere.
lines.note: The word 'khainge' is ambiguous (literally from 'khana' = 'to eat') and here likely slang for 'mess with/attack.' 'Mohde naal' means 'in front of me.' Translation errs on a conservative, literal rendering.

lines.original: Hatth vi jeh lage sadda naam badla di
lines.translated: Even the hands seem like they're for taking revenge for our name.
lines.note: The word 'badla' can mean 'revenge' or 'change'—here it likely means 'revenge'. 'jeh' is a colloquial particle meaning 'as if' or 'sort of', giving a tentative sense.

lines.original: Phir dharla ni haaye
lines.translated: I won't be scared again, oh
lines.note: ‘dharla’ likely derives from Punjabi/Hindi for being scared or cowed; could alternatively imply 'won't back down' depending on context. 'haaye' is an exclamatory interjection.

lines.original: Baal vi jatt da muchh jaye ni
lines.translated: Not even a hair from a jatt's mustache will go.
lines.note: “jatt” refers to a tough Punjabi man/identity (see glossary). The line is a boastful claim — literally that not even a hair of his moustache will be lost or taken.

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased
lines.note: 'rabb' means God; 'raaji re' is a colloquial blessing meaning 'be pleased' or 'be content.'

lines.original: Baaki duniyan russ jaye ni
lines.translated: Let the rest of the world get upset.
lines.note: 'russ jaye' = 'get upset' or 'sulk'; phrase expresses indifference to others' displeasure. 'ni' is an emphatic particle.

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased
lines.note: 'rabb' means God; 'raaji re' is a wish/blessing—asking that God be content or pleased.

lines.original: Baaki duniyan russ jaye ni
lines.translated: Let the rest of the world be upset
lines.note: 'russ jaye' means 'become upset/hold a grudge'; the final 'ni' is a colloquial particle used for emphasis, not a negation.

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased

lines.original: Baaki duniyan russ jaye ni
lines.translated: Let the rest of the world be upset

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji re
lines.translated: May our God be pleased with us

lines.original: Baaki duniyan russ jaye ni
lines.translated: Let the rest of the world get upset.
lines.note: 'russ jana' means 'to sulk / be upset'; 'baaki duniyan' = 'the rest of the world' or 'everyone else'. 'ni' is a colloquial particle used for emphasis.

lines.original: Sadda rabb raaji rahe
lines.translated: May our God remain pleased with us
